title: Accept (and ignore) SystemVerilog unique/priority if.
body:
Add support to the `read_verilog -sv` parser to validate the `unique`, `unique0`, and `priority` keywords in contexts where they're legal according to 1800-2012, section 12.4.2.

This affects _only_ the grammar accepted; the behaviour of conditionals is _not_ changed.  (But accepting this syntax will provide scope for possible optimisations as future work; see below.)

Three test cases ("unique_if", "unique_if_else", and "unique_if_else_begin") verify that the keywords are accepted where legal, and rejected where illegal, as described in the final paragraph of 12.4.2.

_What are the reasons/motivation for this change?_
Two reasons: to improve compatibility with SystemVerilog 1800-2012 (by accepting `unique if` and similar constructions permitted by the spec, which are currently rejected as syntax errors); and to allow future optimisations of conditionals (see below).

_Explain how this is achieved._
This is achieved by modifying the grammar to allow the `unique`/`unique0`/`priority` keywords before an `if`.  (Similar to the existing support when applied before `case`.)  Please note that the keywords are parsed, but *not yet* converted to attributes in the `AstNode`: this is deliberate, to make as small a change as possible with no semantic effects.  The parser does introduce a new `promoted_if` attribute on some `AstNode`s it builds, to distinguish them from the construction that could have been generated by an equivalent `case` statement: this is necessary to correctly reject (e.g.) the illegal `... else unique if ...` syntax.

_If applicable, please suggest to reviewers how they can test the change._
`make test`; verify that the three newly added tests all succeed (correctly accepting the two valid snippets and observing the expected error for the invalid one); and that all other regression tests are unaffected.

_NB:_
If this PR and the general approach is acceptable, I'll plan to add the corresponding _semantic_ support by applying `full_case` and `parallel_case` attributes where appropriate, similarly to the current `case` implementation.  The approach I intend to take is basically flattening a series of `...else if...` branches into a single `AST_CASE/AST_COND.../AST_DEFAULT` construction, rather than the nested `AST_CASEs` currently generated.
